hook: A 1591 alley of cobblers and the poorest, saved from demolition by an antique dealer.
facts: Laid out in 1591 off Oude Koornmarkt 16
Home to cobblers who rang the cathedral's alarm bell
Slated for demolition in the 1960s
Bought and restored by antique dealer Axel Vervoordt
w: Step through an unremarkable gate at Oude Koornmarkt 16 and the traffic falls away. Cobblers lived here in 1591, ringing the cathedral's alarm bell; the poorest of the poor shared the gutters. The 1960s wanted it flattened until an antique dealer bought the lot. A medieval city held its breath.

hook: Eight kilometres of medieval canals, vaulted over as open sewers, now walkable below the streets.
facts: Medieval canals once formed an 8 km water system
Vaulted over across 250 years from the 16th century
Became stinking open sewers driving the cover-up
Opened to the public in 2005; tours start at Ruihuis, Suikerrui 21
w: The whole medieval city once floated on canals, eight kilometres of them, until tanneries and breweries turned the water to stinking soup. Antwerp spent 250 years bricking the lot over. Now you wade and boat beneath your own feet, torch in hand. The sewer as cathedral of forgetting.

hook: A 16th-century printing dynasty's house, presses and library, the only museum on UNESCO's list.
facts: Founded by printer Christophe Plantin in 1576
Holds the two oldest surviving printing presses in the world
Sole museum inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage list
Library and type collection kept intact in the family home
w: The Plantin clan printed bibles and dictionaries here for three centuries, and never threw anything out: presses, lead type, proofs, the lot. Two of the oldest surviving printing presses on earth still stand in the workshop. The only museum UNESCO ever crowned. Ink, vellum and family stubbornness.

hook: An entire boulevard of fin-de-siecle mansions, nearly bulldozed for offices in the 1960s.
facts: Built between 1894 and 1906 in the Zurenborg quarter
Named after John Cogels and baroness Josephine Osy
Forms the 'Golden Triangle' with Transvaalstraat and Waterloostraat
Threatened with demolition for offices in the 1960s, then listed
w: Speculators threw up a whole boulevard of show-off mansions here around 1900, each outdoing its neighbour: a sunflower facade, the four seasons, houses named for continents. The 1960s wanted to raze it for offices. Antwerp listed the lot instead. Suburban one-upmanship, frozen in stucco and wrought iron.

hook: Working wooden escalators sink you under the Scheldt through a 1933 tiled tube.
facts: Built 1931-1933, 572 metres long, 31 metres deep
Original 1930s wooden escalators still in daily use
Opened by King Albert I with 20,000 schoolchildren
Damaged by retreating German troops, reopened 1949
w: Ride one of the last working wooden escalators on earth down into a white-tiled tube, then walk under the river to the far bank and back. Built in 1933, opened by the king and twenty thousand schoolchildren. The Germans wrecked it on their way out in 1944. Art Deco plumbing for pedestrians.

hook: A corner bruin cafe on the Grote Markt whose clock sits forever at five to twelve.
facts: Building called 'Den Engel' since the 14th century
Cafe trading here since 1903
Serves the local bolleke of De Koninck ale
One of the oldest preserved buildings in the city
legend: They say the clock is kept at five to twelve so you are never too late getting home.
w: Order a bolleke, the little round glass of De Koninck, at this corner cafe and watch the clock that never moves past five to twelve. The building has carried the name 'Den Engel' since the 1300s; the cafe has poured since 1903. They say the clock stays put so you are never late home. Brown, loud, eternal.

hook: The home of the bolleke, brewing the city's amber ale on the old toll boundary since 1833.
facts: Founded in 1833 on the Antwerp-Berchem boundary
Brews the amber ale drunk citywide as a 'bolleke'
A toll-post hand once marked merchants entering town here
Grounds now hold a chocolatier, cheesemonger and bakery
w: Order a bolleke anywhere in town and this is what arrives, an amber ale brewed on the old city boundary since 1833. A stone toll-post with a raised hand once stood out front, where merchants paid to enter. Now the yard holds a cheesemonger and a chocolatier. The city's house beer, made at home.

hook: A walled village of devout laywomen hidden behind a blind brick wall in the student quarter.
facts: Beguines bought this triangular plot inside the walls in 1545
Church completed by 1547, rebuilt after French occupation
Around forty houses survive, most 17th-century
The last Antwerp beguine died in 1986
w: A long windowless brick wall on the Rodestraat gives nothing away; behind it lies a whole village of devout laywomen who never took vows and could leave whenever they liked. They bought this plot in 1545. Forty houses still stand, the last beguine gone in 1986. Students walk past, never knowing.

hook: The butchers' guildhall, bacon-streaked in brick and stone, now stuffed with old instruments.
facts: Built early 16th century for the Antwerp butchers' guild
Brick-and-stone banding nicknamed for streaky bacon
Bought by the city in 1899, museum opened 1913
Now holds nearly 4,000 musical objects and rare harpsichords
w: The butchers' guild built itself a palace in striped brick and stone, banded like the bacon they sold, and ran the meat trade from it for three centuries. The city bought it in 1899 and filled it, oddly, with music: four thousand instruments, harpsichords among the best anywhere. From cleavers to clavichords.

hook: A girls' foundling home turned museum, its painted porridge bowls outshining its Rubens.
facts: Orphanage for foundling girls from 1552
Building expanded and refaced 1634-1636
Holds polychrome Antwerp porridge bowls, forerunners of Delftware
Also owns paintings by Rubens, Jordaens and Van Veen
w: Abandoned girls were raised here from 1552, each handed a numbered token and a porridge bowl. The bowls survive, painted Antwerp majolica that taught Delft how to do it. There are Rubenses on the walls too, but the cracked foundling dresses and embroidered samplers undo you faster. Charity, catalogued.

hook: A 1908 festival hall gutted by fire, reborn as a gold-leafed shopping cathedral.
facts: Built 1908 as a festival and exhibition hall on the Meir
Destroyed by fire in December 2000
Listed monument, so the shell could not be demolished
Rebuilt to original plans, reopened 2007 with gilded dome
w: A grand 1908 hall of marble stairs and a glass-iron vault, gutted in a single fierce night in December 2000. Listed as a monument, it could not simply be cleared, so they rebuilt it to the old plans and drowned the dome in gold leaf. Now you buy socks beneath it. Faded grandeur, regilded.

hook: A collector's pocket palace built to house one mad Bruegel of a woman storming hell.
facts: Collection of Fritz Mayer van den Bergh, died 1901
Neo-Gothic house built by his mother as a memorial
Holds Bruegel's 'Dulle Griet' (Mad Meg), bought cheaply in 1894
Opened to the public in 1904
legend: Legend casts Dulle Griet as a shrew so fierce she marches off to plunder the mouth of hell itself.
w: A wealthy young collector snapped up a strange Bruegel for almost nothing in 1894: Mad Meg, a sword-swinging crone striding to loot the mouth of hell. He died at 43; his mother built this neo-Gothic jewel-box to keep his hoard together. Legend made Griet a shrew. Bruegel made her unforgettable.

hook: The dockside sheds where two million emigrants were deloused before sailing to America.
facts: Set in the former Red Star Line embarkation buildings
Line carried some two million emigrants 1873-1934
Passengers were medically inspected and disinfected here
Albert Einstein and Irving Berlin among those who sailed
w: Two million people passed through these dockside sheds between 1873 and 1934, scrubbed, deloused and inspected before the Atlantic crossing. A failed medical sent you home. Einstein went through; so did the boy who became Irving Berlin. The museum keeps their luggage and their fear. The waiting room of the New World.

hook: The baroque garden Rubens designed behind his own house, walled off from the busy Wapper.
facts: Garden of the home and studio Rubens built from 1610
Designed as an Italianate baroque garden with a portico
Pavilion and portico appear in his own paintings
House restored as a museum; garden replanted to period style
w: Behind the painter's mansion on the Wapper lies the garden he laid out himself: an Italianate stage of clipped hedges, a stone portico and a pavilion he then painted into his own canvases. Rubens built his fortune and his myth here from 1610. Slip past the studio crowds; the garden keeps its quiet.

hook: The riverside castle gate, oldest building in town, watched over by a leering carved spirit.
facts: Oldest surviving building in Antwerp, stonework from c.1200-1225
Sole remnant of the medieval city castle
Served as a prison for centuries
Carved figure of Semini, a fertility god, set above the gate
legend: Legend holds the carved Semini above the gate a fertility spirit that women once begged for children.
w: The squat stone gate on the quay outlasts everything else in Antwerp, all that survives of the medieval castle, and for centuries a prison. Above its arch leers Semini, a carved fertility spirit; legend says childless women once prayed to him. Jesuits later defaced him. A pagan ghost on a Christian wall.

hook: A bronze hero hurling a severed giant's hand, the gory pun behind the city's name.
facts: Bronze fountain by Jef Lambeaux, unveiled 1887
Depicts Roman soldier Silvius Brabo and the giant Antigoon
Stands on the Grote Markt before the city hall
Brabo flings the giant's hand into the Scheldt
legend: Legend says the giant Antigoon cut off the hands of sailors who would not pay his toll, until Brabo lopped off the giant's own hand and threw it in the river, naming 'hand-werpen', Antwerp.
w: Jef Lambeaux's 1887 bronze catches the exact instant a Roman soldier flings a severed giant's hand toward the river. Legend says the giant Antigoon docked the hands of sailors who dodged his toll, until Brabo paid him back in kind. 'Hand-werpen', hand-throwing, gave the city its name. Civic pride, dismembered.

hook: A free escalator climb through a stacked-warehouse museum to a 360-degree dockland panorama.
facts: Museum aan de Stroom opened 2011 in the old docks
Red sandstone tower of stacked, rotating floors, 60 metres high
Facade studded with 3,000 aluminium hand motifs
Rooftop and ascending boulevard free to the public
w: Skip the tickets and just ride the escalators up through this stacked-warehouse tower in the old docks, floor twisting on floor, hands studding the red stone facade. The roof gives you the whole port, river and city for nothing. Three thousand little Brabo hands climb with you. The free way to read Antwerp.

hook: A theatrical rockery of life-size saints and tormented souls crammed beside a Gothic church.
facts: Calvary garden built outside Sint-Pauluskerk from 1697
Dozens of life-size stone statues of saints and prophets
Includes a grotto of Purgatory with writhing souls
Church holds paintings by Rubens, Van Dyck and Jordaens
w: Wedged against a Gothic church stands a stone theatre of faith: dozens of life-size saints and prophets on an artificial mountain, and below them a grotto of Purgatory where souls writhe in the flames. Begun in 1697, baroque piety at full volume. Inside hang Rubens and Van Dyck; outside, the drama.

hook: A stone-and-iron 'railway cathedral' so lavish locals nicknamed it the spendthrift palace.
facts: Stone terminus built 1895-1905 by Louis Delacenserie
Steel and glass train shed by Clement Van Bogaert
Dubbed the 'railway cathedral' for its domed stone hall
Rebuilt as a four-level through station, completed 2007
w: King Leopold wanted a station to match his ambitions, so Delacenserie raised a domed stone palace in twenty kinds of marble behind an iron-and-glass shed, finished in 1905. Critics called it a spendthrift's folly; everyone else calls it the railway cathedral. Burrow below and trains run on four stacked levels.

hook: A few cramped streets where most of the world's rough diamonds still change hands on a handshake.
facts: Trade clustered in streets by Centraal Station since the 1800s
Long centred on an Orthodox Jewish community of cutters and dealers
Most of the world's rough diamonds pass through here
Deals traditionally sealed with the Yiddish 'mazel und broche'
w: In four guarded streets behind the station, a fortune in stones still moves on a handshake and a Yiddish blessing, 'mazel und broche', luck and benediction. Orthodox Jewish cutters built the trade; Indian and other dealers share it now. Most of the planet's rough diamonds pass through this drab square mile. Trust as currency.
